title_en: Al-Dasuqi and His Hashiyah: The Definitive Maliki Supercommentary
content_en:
Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn 'Arafah al-Dasuqi (1230–1230 AH approximately / d. 1815 CE) was a leading Maliki scholar and student of the great ad-Dardiri at al-Azhar in Cairo. His name is derived from the town of Dasuq in the Nile Delta region of Egypt. Though his life and scholarship are inseparable from those of his teacher, al-Dasuqi established an independent scholarly reputation through the work for which he is universally known: the Hashiyat al-Dasuqi ala ash-Sharh al-Kabir.

A hashiyah (literally 'margin note' or supercommentary) is a genre of Islamic scholarly writing that comments on a commentary — sitting, so to speak, one level above it in the chain of interpretation. Al-Dasuqi's hashiyah comments on ad-Dardiri's Al-Sharh al-Kabir, which itself comments on Mukhtasar Khalil. This three-level structure — Mukhtasar, Sharh, Hashiyah — is characteristic of mature Sunni fiqh scholarship and reflects the accumulation of centuries of legal analysis layered on top of foundational texts.

The Hashiyat al-Dasuqi is distinguished from many other hashiyahs by the quality and independence of its analysis. Rather than simply glossing difficult words or filling in unexplained references, al-Dasuqi frequently identifies points where ad-Dardiri's explanation needs refinement, notes disagreements among Maliki scholars that his teacher did not fully engage, introduces evidence from earlier Maliki masters, and occasionally defends positions different from those of his teacher. This makes the hashiyah a genuine contribution to Maliki scholarship rather than merely a pedagogical aid.

The Maliki school, in which al-Dasuqi operated, traces its origins to Imam Malik ibn Anas (93–179 AH / 711–795 CE) of Medina. Among its methodological hallmarks is the use of the practice of the people of Medina ('amal ahl al-Madinah) as a source of legal authority — the idea that the continuous practice of the Medinan community, which had lived under the direct teaching of the Prophet and his companions, constitutes a form of transmitted evidence. This principle gives Maliki fiqh a distinctive sociological dimension: law is not only a matter of textual interpretation but of living tradition.

By al-Dasuqi's time, the Maliki school was dominant across North Africa (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya), sub-Saharan West Africa, Egypt, and parts of the Arabian Peninsula, particularly the Hejaz. The school's flexibility in using maslaha (public interest) and 'urf (custom) made it well-suited to diverse cultural and geographic contexts — a quality that enabled its spread across vastly different societies.

The Dasuqi-Dardiri pair — hashiyah and sharh — has functioned as the primary reference for Maliki fatwa and judicial decisions in Egypt and North Africa for over two centuries. In al-Azhar's traditional curriculum, these works are studied together; one cannot be properly understood without the other. Law students, muftis, and judges across the Maliki world rely on this pair for authoritative guidance.

Al-Dasuqi also wrote on usul al-fiqh and produced other smaller works, but it is the Hashiyah that defines his legacy. It represents the culmination of classical Maliki scholarship in Egypt — a monument of the mature tradition that synthesizes centuries of Maliki legal thought into a reference that remains indispensable in legal education and judicial practice across the Islamic world.
